one_line_takeaway: Shows (via reductions to existing binary PRCs) that undetectable, edit-robust LLM watermarking can be extended from high-entropy to low-entropy (single-bit) large-alphabet outputs, at the cost of weaker/heuristic robustness for deletions and edits — a purely theoretical result with no empirical evaluation.
key_finding: The paper's own theorems (conditional reductions, later instantiated in corollaries) establish computationally-undetectable large-alphabet watermarking under only O(1) per-token entropy — a constant δ fraction of tokens each with a single bit of empirical entropy — versus the Ω(log|T|) entropy needed by prior undetectable-robust schemes. Concretely: robustness to a (1/2−ε) fraction of random substitutions assuming subexponential LPN (via [CG24]'s PRC); robustness to (1/2−ε) random substitutions plus a (1−ε) fraction of random deletions given the heuristic "natural prompt" assumption (again from [CG24]'s PRC), or without that heuristic if a PRC robust to adversarial substitutions + random deletions existed (none is currently known); and robustness to a small constant fraction ε′=Õ((δ′)^4) of ARBITRARY/adversarial edits (insertions+deletions) assuming the non-standard "permuted codes" conjecture of [CGG+25]. Supporting quantitative facts from the proofs: each token with empirical entropy ≥1 has success probability ≥5/8 (failure ≤3/4, split ≥1/4), yielding an embedding error rate ≈(1/2 − δ/8). These are theoretical parameters, not measured results — the paper reports no experiments.
core_contribution: A theory/cryptography paper (Mazor, Morgan, Pass; cs.CR, Apr 2026) on provably-undetectable, robust watermarking of LLM outputs. Its own contribution is a construction/reduction that watermarks LLMs with a LARGE token alphabet while requiring only very LOW per-token entropy — namely a "minimal empirical entropy" condition where merely a constant fraction of tokens in a sufficiently long substring have a single bit (O(1)) of empirical entropy, independent of alphabet size or security parameter. This weakens the entropy requirement of prior undetectable schemes, which needed Ω(log|T|) (a constant entropy RATE proportional to log of the token-alphabet size, and for Golowich-Moitra Ω(log λ)). Crucially, the paper does NOT construct new pseudorandom error-correcting codes (PRCs): its theorems are reductions of the form "if a binary PRC with robustness property X exists, then a large-alphabet low-entropy watermarking scheme with property Y exists," and its corollaries instantiate these using PRCs borrowed as black boxes from prior work (Christ-Gunn [CG24] under subexponential LPN, and [CGG+25] under a permuted-codes conjecture). The scheme also introduces a "natural prompt / consistently distributed errors" assumption, a generalization to large alphabets of [CG24]'s "sufficiently variable" heuristic.
threat_model: Two adversaries, both key-less. (1) Robustness adversary: sees the watermarked token sequence and applies an editing channel — random or adversarial substitutions, deletions, and/or insertions (formalized as p-bounded substitution/edit channels and random substitution/deletion channels over a large token vocabulary) — attempting to strip the watermark so AI-generated text passes as human-written; the paper's substitution channel lets the adversary a priori map each token to an arbitrary replacement distribution, capturing paraphrasing/synonym and redaction attacks, and explicitly discusses the "emoji attack" from [KGW+23]. (2) Undetectability adversary: a probabilistic poly-time oracle adversary that must not distinguish watermarked outputs from genuine model outputs. The detector holds a secret key; the LLM developer/watermarker is trusted. The security goal is provenance/detection of AI-generated content, i.e., watermarking as a defense.
limitations: All results are conditional on the existence of a binary PRC with the required robustness (the paper constructs no PRCs itself). Deletion robustness needs EITHER the heuristic "natural prompt / consistently distributed errors" assumption — which the authors themselves argue is unrealistic for natural language, whose tokens are correlated and have non-uniform, often low entropy — OR a PRC robust to adversarial substitutions plus a large fraction of random deletions, for which no construction is currently known. The edit-robust result rests on the non-standard "permuted codes" conjecture of [CGG+25], yields only a very small constant edit fraction (ε′=Õ((δ′)^4)), and requires a longer minimal-entropy substring (a constant fraction of the entire output rather than n blocks). There is no empirical evaluation on real LLMs or real attacks; robustness is asymptotic/negligible-probability in the security parameter. The authors flag as an open problem obtaining low-entropy large-alphabet watermarking robust to a large (e.g., 1−ε) fraction of even random deletions without heuristic assumptions.

quantitative_results: Per-token entropy requirement reduced to O(1) — a constant δ fraction of tokens each with a single bit (≥1) of empirical entropy — vs prior O(log|T|) (Ω(log λ) for [GM24]); see Figure 1
Robustness to a (1/2−ε) fraction of random substitutions for any constant ε>0, assuming subexponential LPN (Theorem 5 / its corollary, via [CG24]'s PRC)
Robustness to (1/2−ε) random substitutions plus a (1−ε) fraction of random deletions, given the heuristic 'natural prompt' assumption and subexponential LPN (Theorem 6 / corollary)
Robustness to a constant ε′=Õ((δ′)^4) fraction of ARBITRARY/adversarial edits assuming the 'permuted codes' conjecture of [CGG+25] (Theorem 8 / corollary)
Each token with empirical entropy ≥1: failure probability ≤3/4, success probability ≥1/4 split → overall embedding success ≥5/8; embedding error rate ≈(1/2 − δ/8)
Averaging argument: with probability ≥1/4 over the hash function h, at least 1/4 of high-entropy tokens are 'split', bounding per-block error rate at ≤1/2 − δ′/16 (Claim 3)
No experiments / no measured/empirical numbers — all quantities are theoretical parameters in asymptotic (negligible-in-n) statements
baselines_compared: Christ-Gunn-Zamir [CGZ24] — first undetectable WM, supports low/sub-constant entropy but only substring-completeness (no robustness to any constant fraction of edits) [cited prior work, compared theoretically in Figure 1]
Christ-Gunn [CG24] — binary-alphabet PRC framework, needs O(log|T|) entropy [cited; its PRCs are reused as black boxes]
Golowich-Moitra [GM24] — large-alphabet edit-robust PRC (1−ε adversarial edits) but needs poly(log λ) / Ω(log|T|) entropy [cited, compared theoretically]
[CGG+25] — permuted-codes PRC robust to adversarial substitutions + small constant fraction of adversarial edits [cited; reused for the edit-robust corollary]
Comparison is analytical, summarized in Figure 1 (entropy vs substitution/edit robustness vs assumptions); no empirical head-to-head was run
